coredata.dc:title: Green synthesis of functionalized fluorescent carbon dots from biomass and their corrosion inhibition mechanism for copper in sulfuric acid environment
coredata.dc:creator: Xu, Zhixiong
Gan, Yue
Zeng, Jie
Chen, Jida
Fu, Anqing
Zheng, Xingwen
Li, Wenpo
coredata.dc:description: The design of new corrosion inhibitors for metallic copper is an important path to ensure that it is not corroded in a wide range of applications such as chemical and electronic industries. In this article, carbon dots with fluorescence properties are synthesized as corrosion inhibitors from green biomass through the hydrothermal method. This method does not generate a large number of waste solvents and toxic byproducts, and has excellent potential to replace other methods. Structural characterizations reveal that the biomass carbon dots (BCDs) contain a significant number of heteroatoms (such as N and S) and heteroatom-containing functional groups, which align with the general properties of corrosion inhibitors. The electrochemical analysis demonstrates an impedance value of 6314.1 Ω cm2 in a 50 mg/L BCD solution at 298 K, significantly higher than that observed in the blank solution, resulting in a corrosion inhibition efficiency of 92.97%. Morphological characterizations reveal that BCDs protect copper through adsorption and coordination film mechanisms. BCDs physically adsorb onto the copper surface and chemically coordinate with Cu+ to form a dense and ordered protective film. Notably, we utilize the fluorescence properties of BCDs as a probe to investigate the protective mechanisms of corrosion inhibitors on metals. Overall, the BCDs show great potential as eco-friendly corrosion inhibitors for copper protection, offering a new direction in corrosion research and practical applications.

head.ce:title: Green synthesis of functionalized fluorescent carbon dots from biomass and their corrosion inhibition mechanism for copper in sulfuric acid environment

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The design of new corrosion inhibitors for metallic copper is an important path to ensure that it is not corroded in a wide range of applications such as chemical and electronic industries. In this article, carbon dots with fluorescence properties are synthesized as corrosion inhibitors from green biomass through the hydrothermal method. This method does not generate a large number of waste solvents and toxic byproducts, and has excellent potential to replace other methods. Structural characterizations reveal that the biomass carbon dots (BCDs) contain a significant number of heteroatoms (such as N and S) and heteroatom-containing functional groups, which align with the general properties of corrosion inhibitors. The electrochemical analysis demonstrates an impedance value of 6314.1 Ω cm2 in a 50 mg/L BCD solution at 298 K, significantly higher than that observed in the blank solution, resulting in a corrosion inhibition efficiency of 92.97%. Morphological characterizations reveal that BCDs protect copper through adsorption and coordination film mechanisms. BCDs physically adsorb onto the copper surface and chemically coordinate with Cu+ to form a dense and ordered protective film. Notably, we utilize the fluorescence properties of BCDs as a probe to investigate the protective mechanisms of corrosion inhibitors on metals. Overall, the BCDs show great potential as eco-friendly corrosion inhibitors for copper protection, offering a new direction in corrosion research and practical applications.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Copper is a strategically significant metallic material and is widely utilized as an advanced composite in chemical equipment, pickling, electroplating, and other industrial sectors due to its outstanding ductility, thermal conductivity and weldable ability [1–3]. However, the issue of copper corrosion, especially in sulfuric acid environments, demands attention. The widespread occurrence of copper corrosion in industries such as heat exchanger pipes, power plant cooling systems, and copper smelting significant challenges to industrial production and equipment reliability. Organic compounds are widely employed as inhibitors in H2SO4 solutions, primarily due to their incorporating nitrogen (N), sulfur (S), and oxygen (O), along with conjugated double bonds and aromatic rings [4–6]. In general, these organic compounds exhibit notable physicochemical characteristics that facilitate significant adsorption, particularly due to the electron-donating nature of the donor atoms and the presence of π orbitals [7]. Furthermore, organic compounds interact with cations dissolved on the metal surface to form metal complexes, which are helpful to cover the metal surface [8,9]. Thus far, a large number of corrosion inhibitors for copper have been reported, including indazole derivatives [10,11], piperazine derivatives [12], and benzotriazole [13,14]. If these inhibitors are utilized frequently, the environment will get contaminated. As environmental preservation has been profoundly ingrained in the minds of the public, green chemistry, particularly nontoxic, eco-friendly, and reproducible green chemistry, has assumed a central role in corrosion inhibitor research. Plant extracts from stems, roots, leaves, and fruits are also utilized as copper corrosion inhibitors; however, the extraction method is difficult and requires organic solvents, whose recycled utilization has also drawn a lot of attention [15].

Fluorescent carbon dots (CDs) garner considerable attention from scientists in various fields such as bio-imaging, energy storage, photocatalysis, and biochemistry [16,17]. The zero-dimensional carbon nanomaterials are highly water-soluble, exhibit exceptional biocompatibility, and can be easily functionalized [18–20]. CDs synthesis mainly adopts two methods [21–23]: “bottom-up” and “top-down”. In general, the “top-down” method involves chemical or physical exfoliation and fragmentation of various sp2 carbon materials. Chemical oxidation is generally more efficient than physical oxidation, which is due to the strong oxidation process that employs a strong oxidant (KClO3) [24,25]. However, the heavy pollution of the products and by-products from the chemical process (such as inorganic salts, harmful gases, and waste acid) makes the chemical “top-down” method unfriendly to the environment and goes against the original intention of green chemistry. As a result, the “bottom-up” approach has received a lot of interest due to how easily CDs can be synthesized. “bottom-up” method includes the dehydrogenation and carbonization of small molecules in a hydrothermal or solvothermal environment [26–28]. Natural biomass materials are rich in carbon sources (fruits, peels, plants, flowers, etc.), and they have great application value, are inexpensive, and are environmentally friendly [29–32]. At the same time, Biomass carbon dots synthesize by the “bottom-up” method using biomass as raw materials are easy to functionalize and very soluble in water, as it have several and different functional groups, such as amine or carboxylic groups and polar moieties on their surface, which is helpful to enhance the anti-corrosion effect on metal materials [33,34]. Therefore, it can be asserted that the synthesis of carbon dots from biomass is truly green corrosion inhibitor, which is not only environmentally friendly but also have the potential to significantly inhibit metal corrosion. Furthermore, there has been little research on biomass carbon dot as a corrosion inhibitor for Cu/H2SO4 systems, so it is critical to realize green biomass carbon dot preparation and study its corrosion inhibition performance.

Herein, the hydrothermal approach is employed to synthesize novel biomass carbon dots with good biocompatibility and high corrosion inhibition efficiency. The BCDs are fabricated from mango pulp that was treated in a hydrothermal environment under a platinum (Pt) catalyzed condition [26]. In contrast to the previous studies that used synthetic organic compounds as carbon sources for CDs synthesis, such as Cui et al. [35] synthesized carbon dots using p-phenylenediamine and o-phenylenediamine through solvothermal methods in ethanol, and the work of Zeng et al. [36] used melamine-modified carbon dots as a highly effective corrosion inhibitor for Q235 carbon steel in a neutral 3.5 wt% NaCl solution. These methods suffer from the widespread use of drugs or organic solvents, high cost, and toxic residues, as well as difficult solvent recycling, generation of significant organic solvent waste, and potential risks to laboratory personnel. Our green synthesis method avoids the problems of traditional chemical synthesis methods. BCDs exhibit excellent fluorescence properties and outstanding water solubility, which was utilized as a fluorescent probe for studying the protective mechanisms of corrosion inhibitors against metal corrosion The previous reports on the use of carbon dots for metal corrosion and protection by Cui et al. [37], Yu Zhang et al. [38] and other researchers did not utilize the fluorescent properties of carbon dots. This application offers new perspectives and approaches to the study of metal corrosion inhibition mechanisms. Compared with the previously reported literature, our work has the following advantages: cheapness, environmental friendliness, excellent corrosion inhibition performance and the utilization of fluorescent properties, as shown in Table S1. This study conducted an in-depth investigation into the protective mechanism of corrosion inhibitors and found that these carbon quantum dots can effectively inhibit copper corrosion by protecting copper materials through adsorption and coordination membrane mechanisms.

Sulphuric acid (98% H2SO4), ultra-pure water (18.25 MΩ), copper block (1 × 1 × 1 cm3), A mature mango was purchased at a local supermarket in Chongqing. All materials are used directly without further chemical treatment.

During the synthesis of BCDs, the experimental procedure involved several stages. Firstly, the mango pulp was crushed using a juicer, resulting in 6 g of crushed pulp. Subsequently, the crushed pulp was thoroughly mixed with 20 mL of ultra-pure water. The resulting mixture was then transferred into a Teflon-lined autoclave and subjected to a temperature of 160 °C for 12 h. After the heating process, the solution was allowed to cool naturally. Next, the mixed solution was filtered through a 0.22 μm microporous membrane to remove impurities. The filtered solution was then transferred into a dialysis bag, and the bag was filled with the solution. The dialysis bag, containing the solution, was placed in a beaker filled with ultra-pure water. The water in the beaker was replaced every 8 h, and this process was repeated three times. Following the completion of the dialysis process, the solution was frozen in a refrigerator for 12 h and subsequently transferred to a freeze-drying oven. The resulting brown solid represented the synthesized BCDs. The preparation process of BCDs is depicted in Fig. S1.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Configuration of working electrode and acidic solution
ce:section.ce:para: As a control, concentrated sulfuric acid (98%) was diluted to 0.5 mol/L H2SO4 solution with ultrapure water. As an experimental group, BCDs of various qualities were dissolved in a blank solution. The working electrode was composed of copper block (1 × 1 × 1 cm3), sealed with epoxy resin, and one side was reserved to be exposed to corrosive solution for the next experiment.

The morphology and size distribution of BCDs were examined via a transmission electron microscope (TEM). The roughness of the metal surface was examined using Atomic Force Microscopy (AFM), while Scanning Electron Microscopy (SEM) was utilized to analyze the corrosion morphology. The ESCALAB 250Xi instrument was utilized for X-ray Photoelectron Spectroscopy (XPS) analysis. The UV–Vis absorption properties of BCDs were characterized using a UV-3600 spectrophotometer. Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (ART-FTIR) was carried out on Nicolet iS50. The corrosion behavior of BCDs was analyzed through electrochemical experiments, specifically involving open-circuit potential (OCP), AC impedance, and potentiodynamic polarization (PDP).

In this experiment, the main electrochemical methods are Open Circuit Potential (OCP), (Electrochemical Impedance Spectroscopy) EIS and Potentiodynamic Polarization (PDP). The traditional three-electrode system is employed to perform measurement. The electrode system included a copper working electrode (WE), a saturated calomel reference electrode (RE), and a platinum counter electrode (CE). The copper electrode was submerged in H2SO4 media for 1800 s before the OCP was measured. Once the OCP curve reached stability, the value of the E OCP was recorded. The EIS test signal is a sine wave with a frequency of 105-10-2 Hz. The PDP test was conducted over a potential range of −250 mV to +250 mV (versus E OCP) with a scan rate of 2 mV/s.

The characterization of BCDs specimens was performed by ART-FTIR. The absorption peaks with different wave numbers represent different functional group information. As illustrated in Fig. 1 a, in the powder sample of BCDs, the OH and NH are responsible for the broad absorption peaks at 3292.03 cm−1 [39]. The weak peak at 2929.49 cm−1 is related to the CH bond [40,41]. The weak peaks at 1662.80 cm−1 are the typical double bond absorption peaks for CO and CC [39,42,43]. The peak observed at 1519.86 cm−1 is assigned to CN stretching vibrations [44]. The peaks at 1396.31 and 1019.76 cm−1 are assigned to COO– and CS/C-SO3 [36,42,45], respectively. The 815.8 cm−1 strong peak is attributed to the thiophene structure and the peak at about 769.17 cm−1 is the characteristic peak of the benzene ring CH stretching vibration.

The UV–vis pattern of BCDs as shown in Fig. 1b, the two shoulder peaks appearing at 200–280 nm and the sharp peak at 320 nm are typically assigned to π–π* and n–π* transitions in organic compounds [42,46]. These transitions occur due to electrons in the π orbitals transitioning to the π* orbitals or from the non-valence n orbitals to the π* orbitals, indicating the existence of aromatic rings and non-bonding electrons in functional groups in the sample. Combining the information from the FTIR, it is possible that the n–π* transition of the CN/CS bond may occur at 230 nm, while the π–π* transition at 300 nm may be related to the electronic transitions in the conjugated structure of BCDs.

Fig. 1c shows the XRD spectra of BCDs powder, where a relatively broad diffraction peak appears at 2θ = 22.7°, which is one of the typical features of amorphous carbon quantum dots, and is consistent with the position of the peaks out of carbon quantum dots in the literature [47,48].TEM is a versatile tool used in materials science, chemistry, and biology to image samples with sub-nanometer resolution and study their microstructure, composition, and morphology. In this experiment, the BCDs were dispersed on the ultrathin carbon film for observation. Fig. 1d shows that the synthesized BCDs have a uniform spherical size distribution of about 10 nm, the schematic diagram of the structural composition of BCDs is shown in Fig. 1e.

The bonding information of the BCDs powder was detected by the XPS test. As illustrated in Fig. 2 a, the XPS full spectrum is displayed. It can be seen from the XPS spectrum that there are peaks of carbon, nitrogen, oxygen, sulfur and copper. It is crucial to note the presence of N and S peaks in the BCDs powder. In Fig. 2b three peaks in the C 1s spectrum are visible at 284.8, 286.3 and 287.7 eV, that is, the CC/CC peak at ∼284.8 eV, CN/CO peak at ∼286.3 eV and CO peak at 287.7 eV [37,49]. In the case of N 1s in Fig. 2c is deconvoluted into two main peaks, which are associated with N-C (400.01 eV) and CNH (401.55 eV), respectively. The O 1s peak in Fig. 2d has two sub-peaks at 532.34 and 533.00 eV, which are attributed to CO and COH/COC groups, respectively [50]. The S 2p spectrum in Fig. 2e shows a pair of doublets that can be assigned to the low valence of CS (163.40 eV) and (CS(O)x C) (168.42 eV), respectively [51,52]. With all of the above characteristics combined, it is simple to envision the successful synthesis of BCDs with a uniform spherical size distribution of around 10 nm in diameter and a significant number of heteroatoms and heteroatom-containing functional groups.

The specimens were characterized by ART-FTIR after being soaked in H2SO4 medium containing 50 mg L−1 BCDs at 298 K for 12 h. As shown in Fig. 3 a, compared with the BCDs powder sample, two weak peaks were detected on the copper soaked in BCDs H2SO4 medium at 451.54 cm−1 and 632.79 cm−1 [53–56]. The peak at 451.54 cm−1 may have been caused by the stretching vibration of CuO/CuS, while the peak at 632.79 cm−1 may be associated with the NCu/Cu2O bond. NCu/Cu2O bond. However, the peaks at 1519.86, 1396.31 and 1076 cm−1 in the powder sample did not appear on the surface of the copper block, so it can be inferred that the N, S and other heteroatoms in powder sample react with the copper to form bonds.

The XPS test was employed to detect the bonding information of the copper block after immersion in H2SO4 solution with 50 mg/L BDs for 12 h. In Fig. 3b, the full XPS spectrum is displayed. It can be seen from the XPS spectrum that there are peaks of carbon, nitrogen, oxygen, sulfur and copper. It is crucial to note that the presence of N and S peaks showed that the N and S of the BCDs had been adsorbed onto the copper surface. Three C 1s spectra peaks are discernible at 284.8, 286.34 and 288.64 eV in Fig. 3c, that is, CC/CC peak (284.8 eV), CN peak (286.34 eV) and CO/CO peak (288.64 eV). In the case of N 1s in Fig. 3d is deconvoluted into three main peaks, which are associated with NC (400.15 eV), CNH (401.13 eV) and N-Cu (402.19 eV), respectively. The O 1s peak in Fig. 3e has three sub-peaks at 531.98, 532.60 and 533.34 eV, which are attributed to CuO/Cu2O, CO and COH/COC bonds, respectively. The presence of the Cu2O peak in the O 1s spectrum further suggests that BCDs can chemically adsorb and effectively inhibit the corrosion of copper by forming chemical bonds with Cu+ on the copper surface. The S 2p spectrum in Fig. 3g shows two pairs of doublets that can be assigned to the low valence of S 2p3/2 and S 2p1/2 thiophene bonds (CuS/CS) at 161.97 and 163.23 eV, respectively, and the high value of S 2p3/2 and S 2p1/2 SO bonds (CS(O)x C) at 169.06 and 170.16 eV, respectively [57]. The containing N functional groups and containing S-functional groups present in BCDs can interact with the surface of copper, forming a stable protective interface that hinders the corrosion process. This protective layer prevents direct contact between copper and the corrosive environment, thereby extending the lifespan of copper. These results demonstrate the positive role of BCDs in protecting copper materials from corrosion. The Cu 2p XPS peak is shown in Fig. 3f [2,6,58]. The peaks observed at 933.09 eV and 952.83 eV can be assigned to Cu 2p3/2/Cu (I) and Cu 2p1/2/Cu (I), respectively. Their presence may be due to the presence of cuprous ions and the copper substrate. The peaks at 936.18 eV and 955.72 eV correspond to Cu (II) 2p3/2 and Cu (II) 2p1/2, respectively. The observation of Cu (I) in the XPS spectra suggests that the addition of biomass carbon dots prevents the conversion of Cu+ to Cu2+, although a small amount of Cu2+ may still be present.

Open circuit potential (OCP) variation over time is one of the key metrics for evaluating the performance of corrosion inhibitors. Fig. 4 shows the OCP plots of a copper immersed in H2SO4 medium with various BCDs concentrations and temperatures. Under all conditions, the OCP curve reaches stability at 1800 s. It can be observed from Fig. 4a that in the BCDs containing solution, the open circuit potential exhibits a decreasing trend during the initial 400 s, which may be linked to the dissolution of the oxide on the copper [59]. The eventual stabilization of the potential indicates that the corrosion inhibitor has played a certain role, successfully forming a protective film that prevents further oxidation and corrosion of the copper surface. It is noteworthy that the addition of BCDs resulted in a considerable negative shift in open circuit potential, exceeding 85 mV when compared to the blank solution. This observation suggests that BCDs possess high adsorption and inhibition abilities. Fig. 4c exhibits significant fluctuations in the open circuit potential, indicating that the metal surface is in an unstable state and prone to corrosion, while the relatively stable open circuit potential in Fig. 5 b reflects that the addition of BCDs has a certain inhibitory and retarding effect on the electrochemical reaction.

In Fig. 4, the PDP curves of copper at 298 K were presented with and without varying concentrations of BCDs. According to the curves, it may be deduced that with the increasing concentration of the BCDs, the corrosion potential significantly shifts toward the negative direction compared to the blank solution [60]. Meanwhile, the corrosion current density decreases significantly, suggesting that the addition of the BCDs can successfully prevent the cathodic reaction. In addition, there is a parallel trend in the cathode branches of all PDP curves, indicating that the cathodic reaction mechanism remains the same with the addition of BCDs. It is worth noting that compared to the blank solution, the solution with BCDs added showed a plateau in the anodic branch at −0.05 V, which could be associated to the BCDs adsorb on the copper, forming a dense layer, and passivation occurring in the anodic region. The corrosion potential (E corr), corrosion current density (i corr), anodic tafel slope (β a), and cathodic tafel slope (β c) of the polarization curve parameters were determined by fitting the curves in the vicinity of the corrosion potential, as presented in Table S2. The following equation was employed to calculate the inhibitory efficiency (η) [61,62]:

From the perspective of reaction mechanisms, the cathodic reaction mainly involves the electrochemical process of dissolved oxygen in an H2SO4 solution being reduced to OH– by gaining electrons. These ions then combine with H+ to generate H2O. The first step of the anodic reaction involves the process in which the copper substrate loses one electron and converts it to Cu+. During this process, the valence electron configuration of copper undergoes a transformation from [Ar] 3d104s1 to the stable configuration of Cu+ ([Ar] 3d10). The second step primarily involves the transformation of Cu+ to Cu2+, which requires higher ionization energy to overcome and is thus a slow process. Therefore, reaction (4) is the rate-determining step. In this case, the lone pair electrons and π electrons in BCDs interact with the empty orbitals of Cu+, while the d-orbital electrons of Cu can bond with the empty orbitals of BCDs.

For the purpose of researching the kinetics of BCD adsorption on copper surfaces, we introduced Arrhenius Eq. (7) and transition state Eq. (8) to calculate the relevant kinetic parameters (the apparent activation energy E a, the enthalpy of activation △H a and the entropy of activation △S a.) based on the corrosion rate and temperature relationship [64]. The relevant kinetic parameters are shown in Table S3.

In Fig. 4(g,h), a linear fit was performed with 1000/T as the abscissa and ln R corr and ln(R corr/T) as the respective ordinates. The slopes of the fitted linear equation represent −E a/R and −ΔH a/R, from which the activation energy and activation enthalpy of BCDs adsorbed on copper surface can be calculated. Moreover, The intercept obtained by using ln(R corr/T) as the ordinate can be expressed as lnR/Nh + ΔS a/R, thus obtaining the value of the entropy of activation (ΔS a).

The results reveal that copper has a lower activation energy in a sulfuric acid blank solution. Cu2+ is easily formed in the absence of an oxide film, resulting in a higher copper corrosion rate. When BCDs are added, the BCDs and Cu+ coordinate to produce a protective film that prevents copper corrosion, the forming of the barrier layer slows the rate of corrosion of copper, but the process itself requires energy. Therefore, the addition of the corrosion inhibitor results in an increase in the Ea of copper in the H2SO4 medium. The positive value of activation enthalpy indicates that the copper dissolution process requires the absorption of heat. However, the presence of BCDs results in a higher activation energy (ΔH a), implying that copper dissolution becomes slower and more difficult. In addition, the negative value of ΔS a in both BCDs-containing and BCDs-free systems indicates a reduction in disorderliness before and after the reaction, corresponding to a more ordered reaction. The addition of BCDs enhances the degree of ordering in the reaction, thereby making the entropy change more negative.

EIS is a valuable technique for assessing the effectiveness of corrosion inhibitors and investigating their adsorption and electrochemical behavior on metal surfaces. Fig. 5 displays the Nyquist and Bode plots for a copper electrode treated by H2SO4 medium, with or without BCDs, at a temperature of 298 K. All the curves in the Nyquist plot, depicted in Fig. 5a, are formed by the combination of a capacitive loop and the Warburg impedance. These depressed capacitive loops may be due to the frequency dispersion of the interfacial impedance and the non-uniform film, while the Warburg impedance may be related to the diffusion of Cu2+ into the bulk solution or the adsorption of dissolved oxygen on the surface of the copper substrate [67]. The incorporation of BCDs led to a substantial enlargement of the capacitive loop diameter, and this effect became more prominent as the concentration of BCDs increased. It is indicated that the presence of BCDs can form a protective layer on the copper, protecting it from corrosion. Furthermore, the curves of the BCDs solution and the blank solution are identical, indicating that although the addition of BCDs changed the impedance of the copper, it did not alter its electrochemical properties. It is noteworthy that in the 50 mg/L BCDs H2SO4 medium, the end of the Warburg impedance line becomes very small, showing that the barrier layer inhibits the diffusion of Cu2+ into the bulk solution and the adsorption of dissolved oxygen on the copper surface. In addition, as shown in Fig. 5b, the slopes of log f and log |Z| are close to −1, indicating ideal capacitive behavior. Compared to the blank solution, as the concentration of BCDs increases, the phase angle becomes broader, possibly due to the overlapping of the two peak values of the phase angle, suggesting that there may be two causes for the BCDs to adsorb on the copper surface: one could be the double-layer capacitance (C dl), and the other could be the adsorption and desorption of BCDs at the copper/solution interface [68].

The EIS data from the experiment were examined by fitting them to a classical equivalent circuit (shown in Fig. 5f). The circuit includes several components: solution resistance (R s), film resistance (R f), charge-transfer resistance (R ct), Warburg impedance (W), and constant-phase angle elements (CPEf and CPEdl). The CPE elements are related to the electrical double-layer capacitance (C dl) and film capacitance (C f), respectively. During the curve fitting process, ideal capacitors (C dl, C f) were replaced with CPEf and CPEdl to account for the non-ideal capacitive behavior of the heterogeneous electrode. The following equation can be used to describe the impedance function of the CPE [69].

where Y o represents the constant phase element (CPE) coefficient, j is the imaginary root, ω is the angular frequency, and n is the dispersion coefficient. C f and C dl are calculated using the following formulas:

The η of BCDs on copper in H2SO4 medium is calculated using the following formula [38,70]:

where R p,0 and R p respectively represent the sum of charge transfer resistance (R ct) and film resistance (R f) in the absence and presence of BCDs.

The EIS data was shown in Table S4, it can be observed that the values of C f and C dl in the solution with BCDs added are decreased compared to those in the solution without BCDs. This could be due to a drop in the local dielectric constant and the reduction in exposed copper surface area. Moreover, the thickness of the double-layer capacitance is also increased. We speculate that BCDs replace water molecules, thereby inhibiting the corrosion of copper. This inhibition effect could be ascribed to BCDs forming a barrier layer over the copper surface, thus slowing down the electrochemical reaction rate. Therefore, the decrease in C f and C dl values indicates that adding BCDs can improve the corrosion inhibition performance and reduce copper corrosion [61,71]. It is noteworthy that with an increase in BCDs concentration, the values of R f and R ct also increase, indicating that BCDs molecules may form a protective film. This film can slow down the electrochemical reaction rate of copper in sulfuric acid solution, thereby preventing further corrosion of copper. Following this trend, the inhibition efficiency (η) also increases with an increase in concentration. At 298 K, the inhibition efficiency can reach 92.97% at a BCDs concentration of 50 mg/L. The superior inhibition performance indicates that BCDs can effectively anchor to and adsorb on the copper surface.

Further electrochemical tests were conducted from 288 K to 328 K to investigate the stability of BCDs film formation. Fig. 5c–f displays Nyquist and Bode plots of copper in H2SO4 solutions (with and without BCDs) at different temperatures. The graphs reveal that the capacitance arc radius of both the blank and BCDs containing solutions decrease with increasing temperature, indicating that the corrosion rate of the copper electrode is accelerated by elevated temperatures. In addition, at the same temperature, the impedance radius is significantly larger in the solution containing BCDs compared to the blank solution. According to Table S5, in the blank solution, the values of C f and C dl increased with temperature, while the values of R f and R ct decreased with temperature. This suggests that increasing the temperature accelerated the diffusion rate of the corrosion products and promoted the charge transfer process of the corrosion reaction. With the addition of BCDs, the values of R f and R ct increased, while the values of C f and C dl decreased, indicating that the temperature increase accelerated the thermal motion of BCDs molecules. However, the inhibition efficiency still exceeded 90%, suggesting that although the temperature increase accelerated the corrosion of the copper block, BCDs could also prevent further corrosion of copper by forming a protective layer adsorbed on the copper surface.

As depicted in Fig. 6 a–c, the surface morphologies of copper samples soaked in H2SO4 medium without BCDs at different temperatures. As the temperature increases, the copper surface becomes increasingly rough, with numerous cracks, pits, and more obvious corrosion products, indicating more severe corrosion. However, at the presence of BCDs in Fig. 6d–f, a dense barrier layer was observed on the copper. With the increase of temperature, the cracks in the protective film became larger, which was related to the acceleration of BCDs' thermal motion on the copper surface, leading to the adsorption becoming looser or even disrupted. From the SEM cross-sectional images, it can be seen that a protective film with a thickness of several micrometers was formed on the copper substrate surface, indicating that BCDs are effective at preventing corrosion of the copper.

The atomic microscope is a powerful tool that can observe the roughness of metal surfaces from the nanometer and micrometer scales. Fig. 6g, h showed the AFM results of the copper after 24 h immersion in 0.5 M H2SO4 medium containing and contain-free 50 mg/L BCDs at 298 K. The 3D and 2D AFM patterns in Fig. 6g were rougher than those in Fig. 6h, as can be seen. The surface of copper was severely uneven due to significant sample erosion, and the Ra value increased to 50.308 nm (Fig. S2a). By comparison, the Ra value stupendously decreased to 20.665 nm when 50 mg/L BCDs (Fig. S2b) were added, indicating that the BCDs could retard the corrosion of copper. The results are in line with SEM and electrochemical findings, further demonstrating the effectiveness of BCDs in preventing the sulfuric acid solution from corroding copper.

ce:section.ce:section-title: BCDs as a fluorescent probe for investigating corrosion mechanisms
ce:section.ce:para: Laser Scanning Confocal Microscopy (LSCM) is a high-resolution and high-sensitivity microscopy technique, which relies on laser beam focusing to scan and image the fluorescence signal of the sample surface point by point. Due to the fluorescent property of BCDs, LSCM can be employed to observe the intensity of the fluorescence signal on the copper surface, providing insight into the way BCDs function on the surface of copper. Fig. 7 a–e shows laser scanning confocal microscopy images of copper blocks immersed in sulfuric acid solutions with or without BCDs at 298 K for different periods of time. In Fig. 7a, no fluorescence signal was recorded on the copper surface initially due to the absence of BCDs. After 0.5 h of immersion, some fluorescence signals appeared on the copper surface, indicating that BCDs have a slower rate compared to conventional organic molecules. As the immersion time was prolonged, a growing quantity of fluorescence signals emerged on the surface of the copper. At 12 h, the fluorescence signals covered the entire area of the copper block, as demonstrated in Fig. 7e. The specific adsorption of BCDs on the copper surface can be attributed to the abundant heteroatoms and functional groups present in the BCDs, which is in agreement with the observations obtained from scanning electron microscopy. This finding confirms the film-forming mechanism of BCDs on the copper surface.

The fluorescence spectrophotometer is an analytical instrument that can be used to determine the properties of fluorescent substances. Fig. 7(f–h) further intuitively explores the corrosion inhibition mechanism of BCDs on copper by measuring the change of fluorescence intensity over time with and without adding copper blocks in BCDs solution. Fig. 7g indicates that a strong fluorescence signal was detected when the copper block was initially added, and the fluorescence signal gradually weakened over time. It is noteworthy that before 60 min, the fluorescence intensity of the solution changed greatly, while after 60 min, although the fluorescence intensity decreased to a certain extent, the decrease rate was relatively slow. Additionally, Fig. 7h illustrates that the fluorescence intensity of BCDs solution with the same concentration did not change significantly when the copper block was not added. It can be inferred that the BCDs solution with fluorescence properties adsorbed on the copper surface over time, which reduced the concentration of the solution and thus affected its fluorescence intensity. When the adsorption saturation was reached on the copper surface, the change in fluorescence intensity of the solution was no longer significant. In summary, BCDs undergo a physicochemical reaction on the copper, forming a dense barrier layer, thereby preventing the copper from corroding in H2SO4 media.

Fig. 8 depicts the protection mechanism of BCDs on Cu in H2SO4 medium, based on various experimental data and results. On one hand, BCDs contain abundant heteroatoms and lone pairs of electrons, which can electrostatically interact with the dissolved Cu+ on the surface of copper and be adsorbed onto it. On the other hand, the pyrrolic nitrogen atoms contained in BCDs can be anchored and adsorbed on the Cu surface by transferring electrons to form coordination bonds with empty d orbitals on the copper surface to form NCu bonds. Besides NCu bonds, sulfur atoms in CS/CS bonds in BCDs can also coordinate with Cu to form SCu bonds. XPS and FTIR can prove the existence of SCu and NCu bonds on copper substrate surfaces. In the front and cross-section of the SEM, a dense and orderly barrier film can be seen. When combined with electrochemical data, it can be demonstrated that BCDs form barrier film via physical adsorption and chemical coordination, inhibiting the reaction rate of copper cathode and anode in sulfuric acid solution and thus slowing copper corrosion.

In summary, this study successfully synthesized water-soluble biomass carbon dots via a hydrothermal method and applied them as corrosion inhibitors for copper in H2SO4 medium. The Electrochemical results demonstrate excellent protective effects of BCDs on copper under acidic conditions. Specifically, at a BCD concentration of 50 mg/L and a temperature of 298 K, the corrosion inhibition efficiency of copper reaches 92.77%. Additionally, the electrochemical data provide thermodynamically related parameters. The increase in activation enthalpy indicates a reduction in the corrosion rate of copper in the presence of BCDs in sulfuric acid solution, while the decrease in entropy change reveals a decrease in disorder before and after the reaction. To gain a comprehensive understanding of the film-forming mechanism of BCDs on the copper surface, SEM and AFM morphology tests were performed, and the fluorescent properties of BCDs were utilized as a fluorescence probe for copper in sulfuric acid solution. Through the combination of these methods, we were able to delve deeper into the process of BCDs forming a film on the copper surface through physical adsorption and chemical coordination. In conclusion, this study provides a new perspective for the research and design of novel green corrosion inhibitors. The experimental findings confirm the excellent performance of water-soluble biomass carbon dots synthesized via the hydrothermal method in copper corrosion inhibition. This has significant implications for further optimization of the corrosion inhibition performance of BCDs and a better understanding of their protective mechanism.
